metadata.summary: Decompose approved design into bounded TASK_*.md files using INVEST criteria, SMART task goals, WBS 8-80 rule adapted to token budgets (max 100k tokens per task), Given-When-Then AC, and full FR/AD traceability.

content.title: Task Creation Principles
content.summary: Principles for decomposing an approved design into bounded, executor-ready TASK_*.md files. Applies the INVEST criteria (Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable), SMART task goals, WBS 8-80 rule adapted to token budgets (hard cap: 100k tokens per task), Given-When-Then acceptance criteria, and full FR-X/AD-X traceability links inside each task file.

why.p: Implementation plans fail when tasks are too large (executor runs out of context mid-implementation), too vague (AC are not testable), or untraceable (executor cannot verify the task satisfies a requirement). INVEST + SMART enforced at task creation time catches these defects before execution, and inline FR/AD text eliminates re-reading the full SDD context during execution.

list.item: Converting an approved design document into executable TASK_*.md files
Splitting large undefined work into bounded units before assigning to a subagent
Auditing an existing implementation plan where tasks are vague or oversized
When faion-sdd-executor-agent will run unattended and needs unambiguous inputs
Prototyping or exploratory spikes — INVEST/SMART overhead is not worth it
Single-file hot-fixes with obvious scope — task structure adds ceremony with no return
Early brainstorming phases before a spec exists (no FR-X to trace to yet)

rule.statement: Every task must fit within 100k tokens total (research + SDD docs + implementation + tests + buffer). Tasks exceeding this limit must be split at natural code boundaries.
rule.rationale: Executor agents that run out of context mid-implementation produce partial or inconsistent code. The 100k limit is a hard enforcement boundary, not a suggestion.

example.title: Token tier table for task sizing
example.code: Complexity | Token Range | Description ---------- | ----------- | ----------- simple | <30k | Single file, clear existing pattern normal | 30-60k | Multiple files, some decisions needed complex | 60-100k | Architecture decisions, research needed

example.title: Right size — single responsibility
example.code: Task: "Create password hashing utilities with bcrypt" (~30k tokens, single file)

rule.statement: Each task must satisfy all six INVEST criteria before being assigned to an executor. A task that fails any criterion must be rewritten or split.
rule.rationale: INVEST failures cause execution failures: non-independent tasks block each other unexpectedly, non-testable tasks cannot be verified as done.

example.title: INVEST criteria table with verification questions
example.code: Criterion | Verification Question | Example ------------ | ---------------------------------------- | ------- Independent | Can be done without blocking others? | Password utils (no deps) Negotiable | Can details be refined? | Algorithm choice is flexible Valuable | Clear user or business value? | "Enables secure login" Estimable | Can estimate tokens? | ~30k tokens Small | Fits in one work session? | Not "build auth system" Testable | Has Given-When-Then AC? | AC-1 through AC-4 defined

rule.statement: Task Objective must satisfy SMART: one interpretation only (Specific), verifiable by test (Measurable), technically feasible (Achievable), traces to FR-X (Relevant), has a token estimate (bound).
rule.rationale: Non-SMART objectives cause executors to implement the wrong scope — too much, too little, or in the wrong direction.

rule.statement: Every task must include inline FR-X and AD-X text from spec and design. Do not link — paste the full text of each requirement and decision the task implements.
rule.rationale: Executor agents that must re-read the full spec to understand their task add 10-30k tokens of overhead and risk misreading a requirement out of context.

example.title: Traceability section in task file
example.code:
## Requirements Coverage
### FR-2: User can log in with email/password Full text: "System SHALL authenticate users by verifying email and bcrypt-hashed password. Returns JWT on success." This task implements: bcrypt verification in password service.
### AD-1: JWT for authentication Full text: "Use JWT with 7-day expiry, no refresh tokens for MVP. Rationale: stateless, no Redis dependency." This task implements: JWT generation and HTTP-only cookie storage.

rule.statement: Task state is determined by which directory it lives in (todo/ / in-progress/ / done/), not by the Status field in the header. The header status must always match the directory.
rule.rationale: Executor agents check the directory when scanning for work. A task in todo/ is picked up regardless of its Status header value.

list.item: todo/ — Ready to start, all dependencies met
in-progress/ — Being worked on by one executor
done/ — Completed, all AC verified, execution report filled

rule.statement: Every acceptance criterion must use Given-When-Then format with specific values. Vague criteria ("system works correctly") are rejected.
rule.rationale: Executor agents cannot verify vague criteria. Every AC must have an observable, code-verifiable outcome — a test that passes or fails.

example.title: Well-formed acceptance criteria
example.code:
## Acceptance Criteria
**AC-1: Successful login** - Given: registered user with email "test@example.com" and correct password - When: user submits login form - Then: JWT token returned in HTTP-only cookie, user redirected to /dashboard, response 200
**AC-2: Invalid password** - Given: registered user with correct email but wrong password - When: user submits login form - Then: error message "Invalid credentials" shown, no cookie set, response 401
**AC-3: Expired token** - Given: user with a JWT cookie expired 1 second ago - When: user accesses /api/protected - Then: response 401, body {"error": "Token expired"}

rule.statement: For background jobs with no user actor, use "Given / Triggered by / Then" instead of "Given / When / Then".
rule.rationale: Standard GWT requires a "when" actor. Background jobs are triggered by events (cron, queue message), not user actions.

list.item: Happy path (successful scenario with correct inputs)
Alternative paths (valid variations of the happy path)
Boundary conditions (minimum/maximum values, edge cases)
Error handling (invalid inputs, service failures)
Security scenarios (unauthorized access, injection attempts)
Performance (if an NFR applies to this task)
Implementation plan reviewed and task row identified
Design decisions (AD-X) understood — read the full AD text
Requirements (FR-X) mapped — read the full FR text
INVEST criteria all met
SMART criteria all met
Complexity tier assigned (simple/normal/complex)
SDD references included as table
Given-When-Then format throughout
Happy path covered
Error cases covered
Boundary conditions covered
Each criterion is testable — not vague
FR-X text pasted inline (not linked)
AD-X text pasted inline (not linked)
Token estimate <100k with breakdown
Dependency tree populated from actual done/ task files

antipattern.description: Tasks too large — "Implement authentication" as a single task.
antipattern.reason: Break down until each task fits one work session and <100k tokens.

antipattern.description: No traceability — FR-X and AD-X not mentioned in task.
antipattern.reason: Add FR-X and AD-X links inline; executor cannot verify the task satisfies a requirement without them.

antipattern.description: Vague acceptance criteria — "user experience is smooth".
antipattern.reason: Replace with Given-When-Then that a test can verify as pass or fail.

antipattern.description: Missing test files in Files table.
antipattern.reason: Include testing in AC and list test files explicitly; tests are required, not optional.

antipattern.description: INVEST "Independent" criterion violated in sequential migration chains.
antipattern.reason: Accept partial dependency and document it explicitly — forcing artificial independence causes worse splits.

section.name: agent-integration-workflow
section.p: A planning agent reads the approved design.md and spec.md, then produces one TASK_*.md file per unit. Each file is validated against the INVEST/SMART checklist before being written. The executor agent (faion-sdd-executor-agent) later picks tasks from todo/ and runs them sequentially in dependency order, writing an execution report back into the same file.
Keep task creation and task execution in separate agent turns: a planning agent that also executes tends to cut corners on AC definition.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — executes tasks from todo/, moves through lifecycle, writes execution reports
General Claude subagent (Sonnet) — sufficient for task decomposition from a well-structured design doc

example.title: Task decomposition prompt
example.code: Given this design.md and spec.md, produce TASK_*.md files. Each task: <100k tokens, INVEST-valid, Given-When-Then ACs, FR/AD links. Output one markdown block per task. Do not implement anything.

example.title: Task review prompt
example.code: Review these TASK_*.md files. Flag any that violate INVEST (not independent, not estimable, not testable). For each violation, suggest a split or rewrite.

list.item: tokencounter / ttok — Estimate token count of a file before assigning. Install: pip install ttok. Docs: https://github.com/simonw/ttok
jq — Parse and validate JSON task metadata if stored in frontmatter. System package.
grep / ripgrep — Trace FR-X and AD-X references across docs. Install: apt install ripgrep
Linear (SaaS, Agent-friendly) — Can create issues from TASK_*.md; maps to INVEST story format. REST + GraphQL API.
GitHub Issues (SaaS, Agent-friendly) — Cheapest integration; label by complexity; milestone = wave. gh CLI + REST.
Jira (SaaS, Partial) — Overkill for solo workflow; use only if client requires. REST API, complex auth.
Notion (SaaS, Partial) — Good for stakeholder visibility; not ideal for agent writes. REST API.
Write task files in waves: create Wave 1 tasks in detail, execute, then write Wave 2 with patterns learned from Wave 1 output
Always include the full text of referenced FR-X and AD-X inline in the task file — the executor agent should not need to re-read the entire spec
Keep "Technical Notes" specific: name the exact pattern file, line range, or prior task that establishes the pattern to follow
One task = one commit; never combine multiple TASK-IDs in a single commit
Assign complex tasks their own wave rather than parallelizing them — complex tasks have higher abort/rework rates
Executor agents ignore vague AC like "works correctly" — every criterion must have an observable, code-verifiable outcome
If a task file is a stub ("See implementation-plan.md"), faion-sdd-executor-agent extracts from the plan table — this loses context; always prefer full task files
Circular dependency detection is not automatic — validate the dependency graph manually before handing to an executor
Agents tend to overestimate independence: "no code dependency" does not mean "no data dependency" (e.g., task B needs the DB rows created by task A)
Human checkpoint required before executing any complex-rated task that touches auth, payments, or public APIs — flag these in the task file
INVEST "Independent" criterion is often violated in sequential migration chains — accept partial dependency and document it rather than forcing artificial splits
Token estimates are rough; large config files or generated types can spike context unexpectedly
Given-When-Then ACs work poorly for performance NFRs — supplement with measurable thresholds rather than scenario language
